result.chapter_summary: Chapter 8 proposes a communal, knowledge-based curriculum as the necessary solution for American education, arguing that such a curriculum is the historical foundation of successful modern democracies. It asserts that to achieve social justice, schools must provide all students with the 'language of power'—the traditional and evolving knowledge shared by the citizenry—rather than abandoning content in favor of abstract skills.

claims.text: The United States invented both the modern democratic nation and the common school system that sustained it.
claims.source_passage: Historically, the United States invented both the modern democratic nation and the common school that sustained it.

claims.text: Schooling in the United States was designed to advance a knowledgeable citizenry and social cohesion through commonality.
claims.source_passage: Unity was to be created not just by a system of laws but also by a system of schooling that advanced a knowledgeable citizenry and social cohesion.

claims.text: School-based commonality is the foundation of all modern democracies.
claims.source_passage: School-based commonality is now the foundation of modern democracies everywhere.

claims.text: Communication and community facilitated through common schools is the defining characteristic of a successful modern nation.
claims.source_passage: Communication and community through the common school is the hallmark of the successful modern nation.

claims.text: The Core Knowledge Sequence is an example of an enabling elementary curriculum, not the only possible communal curriculum.
claims.source_passage: The Core Knowledge Sequence is put forward only as an example of what such a curriculum looks like in the American context.

claims.text: At present, Core Knowledge is the only published outline of a complete, enabling elementary curriculum for American schools.
claims.source_passage: At the moment, it is the only published outline of a complete, enabling elementary curriculum for American elementary schools.

claims.text: Alternative communal curricula must be created because Americans demand curricular choice and reject imposed uniformity.
claims.source_passage: For widespread progress to be made, alternative communal curriculums will need to be created, since Americans do not like the idea of imposed uniformity. They demand a choice.

claims.text: Any curriculum that successfully imparts Standard American English through its enabling shared knowledge would be effective.
claims.source_passage: Any broad curriculum that successfully delivers command of Standard American English by means of the standard American knowledge that enables its use would be equally effective.

claims.text: Shared knowledge is an essential prerequisite for success in American life.
claims.source_passage: That shared knowledge must be mastered by young pupils if they are to have a good chance at success in American life.

claims.text: A communal curriculum must include material that American communicators take for granted in their audience.
claims.source_passage: Such a communal curriculum must include a lot of material that American writers and speakers have long taken for granted in their readers and listeners.

claims.text: Knowledge shared among millions of people possesses an inherent and inescapable inertia.
claims.source_passage: Because of an inherent and inescapable inertia in the knowledge that is shared among hundreds of millions of people, the Core Knowledge plan was necessarily traditional.

claims.text: The aim of providing all students access to the 'knowledge of power' conflicts with intellectual movements that seek to deprivilege dominant cultures.
claims.source_passage: The aim of giving everybody entrée to the knowledge of power ran smack up against the aim of deprivileging those who are currently privileged.

claims.text: Social justice requires teaching traditional knowledge while simultaneously adding new cultural elements.
claims.source_passage: How to solve that practical and moral impasse? Only by teaching traditional knowledge in elementary school, while adding in new elements.

claims.text: Students cannot master the existing language of power and success if they are taught only a non-traditional knowledge set.
claims.source_passage: If we tried to teach children a fully nontraditional knowledge set, they could not master the existing language of power and success.

claims.text: The failure of most schools to teach the language of power to disadvantaged children keeps them at a severe disadvantage.
claims.source_passage: Disadvantaged children currently do not master it in most of our schools, and that puts them at a continued severe disadvantage.

claims.text: Vocabulary in literate society changes at a slow rate despite the perception of rapid technological change.
claims.source_passage: If we look at what has actually happened to the vocabularies of literate adults in contemporary America, new words and names have come into currency... but at a rather slow rate.

claims.text: The inclusion of figures like Rosa Parks in canonical knowledge is the result of deliberate cultural activism.
claims.source_passage: The addition of Rosa Parks to canonical knowledge has been the deliberate work of cultural activists.

claims.text: The linguistic scene in the United States and other modern nations resembles an 'amoeba' that is constantly changing its shape through word usage.
claims.source_passage: The whole linguistic scene in the United States and other modern nations begins to look like an amoeba that is changing its shape. Some words enter usage and memory, others depart

claims.text: Cultural activists have successfully replaced traditional figures like John Jay with figures like Rosa Parks in the contemporary American canonical mind.
claims.source_passage: Rosa Parks has pushed out John Jay in the contemporary American mind. The addition of Rosa Parks to canonical knowledge has been the deliberate work of cultural activists

claims.text: The core vocabulary and knowledge of a language is relatively stable, changing by only about 1 percent over a decade.
claims.source_passage: Webster’s Collegiate has introduced 1,700 new entries out of about 170,000—a change of 1 percent in a decade... a change of 1 percent in a decade is not dizzyingly fast.

claims.text: There is a stable nucleus of knowledge at the center of language that does not readily change shape or constitution.
claims.source_passage: At the center of the amoeba there is a nucleus that does not readily change shape or constitution.

claims.text: Most foundational knowledge required for elementary school instruction is not found in newly introduced vocabulary items.
claims.source_passage: Most of the things we need to teach in elementary schools are not among the 1,700 new items in Webster.

claims.text: Word frequency is an unreliable guide for determining school curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Nor is mere word frequency a good guide to the curriculum.

claims.text: Knowledge with high utility and durability, such as that of Franklin Roosevelt, should be taught even if its usage frequency is lower than contemporary figures like Kim Kardashian.
claims.source_passage: Today the frequency of Franklin Roosevelt is rare compared to Kim Kardashian, but there is good reason to teach Roosevelt, which we estimate to have more utility and durability.

claims.text: Responsible elementary curricula will exhibit a high degree of overlap due to a social obligation to provide children with the knowledge required for American society.
claims.source_passage: Rival elementary curriculums may reflect rival values, but all responsible ones will exhibit a great deal of overlap—out of a sense of obligation to the children who will need the knowledge represented by those words in order to function well in American society.

claims.text: The principle of communal knowledge is far more effective and fair than the individualistic principle of early education.
claims.source_passage: the inadequacy of the individualistic principle of early education is well understood, and is replaced by the far more effective and fair principle of communal knowledge.

claims.text: The effectiveness of an elementary school is determined by the knowledge it imparts rather than its governance structure.
claims.source_passage: The key differences between an effective and an ineffective elementary school reside in the effectiveness of the knowledge they impart, not in their governance structures.

claims.text: The difference between a coherent knowledge curriculum and a 'helter-skelter' skills curriculum is more significant than administrative school arrangements.
claims.source_passage: The difference between a helter-skelter skills curriculum and a coherent knowledge curriculum is far more significant than any differences of administrative school arrangements.

claims.text: Governance structures are not educational structures.
claims.source_passage: Governance structures are not educational structures.

claims.text: The American charter school movement was driven by dissatisfaction with educational results, not dissatisfaction with administrative arrangements themselves.
claims.source_passage: The new administrative structures of the charter school movement in the United States were developed because of our dissatisfaction with the results of our regular public schools, not dissatisfaction with administrative arrangements.

claims.text: Content-specific curricula that work in high-performing nations are equally effective in the United States.
claims.source_passage: The content-specific type of curriculum that has worked well in the school systems of the best-performing nations also works well in the United States.

claims.text: Common curricula do not suppress individual creativity or individuality.
claims.source_passage: The knowledge may be shared, but there is no lack of individuality in the way it is received and portrayed. The walls declare useful refutations of the usual objections to a common curriculum: that it suppresses individuality creativity

claims.text: Accusations that knowledge-based schooling is 'rote-learned' or 'developmentally inappropriate' are untrue caricatures.
claims.source_passage: The claims that a knowledge-based school is defined by 'rote learning of mere facts,' that it is 'purely verbal, not hands-on,' that it is 'developmentally inappropriate'—these are all untrue caricatures

claims.text: Most problems of American schooling, specifically inequality of opportunity, can be solved by requiring content-coherent schooling from preschool through grade 5.
claims.source_passage: Most problems of American schooling (above all, the problem of inequality of opportunity) can be solved if we insist upon content-coherent schooling from preschool through grade 5.

claims.text: A naturalistic, content-delayed, and individualistic approach in early grades guarantees failure for disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: We now know from data here and abroad that a naturalistic, content-delayed, and individualistic approach in those grades consigns disadvantaged students to failure

claims.text: The early grades serve as the 'fulcrum' for determining a student's subsequent life chances.
claims.source_passage: The early grades are the fulcrum of this process; they are where a knowledge and a language base can be consolidated that will determine a student’s subsequent chances in life.

claims.text: The primary school years are the critical period for preventing preschool gain fadeout.
claims.source_passage: The primary school is where preschool fadeout can be prevented; it is where a knowledge and a language base can be consolidated that will determine a student’s subsequent chances in life.

claims.text: The early grades are the fulcrum of the entire school system, particularly for students with initial knowledge and vocabulary deficits.
claims.source_passage: The early grades are the fulcrum of the whole school system, especially for students who have started out with major deficits in knowledge and vocabulary.

claims.text: The substance of what children learn in grades K–3 determines whether preschool gains will endure or fade by the end of grade 4 or 5.
claims.source_passage: Chapter 3 showed, for example, that the substance of what children gradually learn in grades K–3 will determine, by the end of grade 4 or 5, whether preschool gains will take hold rather than fade out.

claims.text: The knowledge deficit in the early grades is a finite and manageable amount that can be overcome through systematic schooling.
claims.source_passage: In the early grades, the finite amount of knowledge to be made up is still manageable. Key deficits can be overcome through good systematic schooling, making it a critical period for overcoming the “Matthew Effect” in learning.

claims.text: It takes knowledge to gain more knowledge, much like it takes money to make money.
claims.source_passage: Another analogy is that of knowledge capital: just as it takes money to make money, it takes knowledge to gain more knowledge.

claims.text: In a common curriculum, disadvantaged students can learn more from each unit than advantaged students, allowing them to catch up.
claims.source_passage: In a carefully conducted primary school, where all students follow a common curriculum in the early grades and are prepared for each lesson by prior lessons, the have-nots can learn even more from each unit than the haves, and they are able gradually to catch up.

claims.text: A systematic approach to knowledge simultaneously prepares advantaged students and enables disadvantaged students to catch up.
claims.source_passage: A well-rounded elementary school thus kills two birds with one stone: it prepares advantaged students to become well educated, and it enables disadvantaged students to continue catching up with them.

claims.text: The primary school is not primarily a place for developing 'basic skills' but for building the knowledge and vocabulary that determine long-term learning ability.
claims.source_passage: The primary school is not just a place where “basic skills” are developed. It is where knowledge and vocabulary are built up that will determine a student’s long-term ability to gain further knowledge and vocabulary.

claims.text: The concept of 'basic skills' as applied to early education is an oversimplification.
claims.source_passage: The concept of “basic skills” is an oversimplification.

claims.text: Middle school and high school readiness are critically dependent on students possessing a broad base of knowledge in specific disciplines (science, history, art, etc.) by age eleven.
claims.source_passage: Then, high school readiness critically depends on whether students’ knowledge and vocabulary is already broadly based in science, architecture, painting, history, civics, geography, music, astronomy, literature, and math.

claims.text: High school is too late for a student to begin taking coherent content seriously if they did not do so in primary school.
claims.source_passage: High school is too late to start taking coherent content seriously.

claims.text: Vocabulary size is a visible proxy for a student's internal acquisition of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Vocabulary size is the outward and visible sign of an inward acquisition of knowledge.

claims.text: Vocabulary size is a reliable correlate to economic class and future annual income.
claims.source_passage: It is also a reliable correlate to economic class. One standard deviation in vocabulary size averages about ten thousand 2012 dollars in annual income.

claims.text: A knowledge-based approach to early education is better at narrowing differences in academic achievement and later earnings than a skills-based approach.
claims.source_passage: The latter is much better at narrowing differences in academic achievement and later earnings.

claims.text: Vocabulary size is an outward and visible sign of an inward acquisition of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: is the outward and visible sign of an inward acquisition of knowledge.

claims.text: One standard deviation in vocabulary size averages about ten thousand 2012 dollars in annual income.
claims.source_passage: One standard deviation in vocabulary size averages about ten thousand 2012 dollars in annual income.

claims.text: Integrated and planned-out curricular coherence speeds up learning and vocabulary gain.
claims.source_passage: Such planned-out coherence speeds up learning and vocabulary gain.

claims.text: Under a common curriculum, students who are behind catch up by getting up to speed by the end of each content unit.
claims.source_passage: Under a common curriculum the children who are behind slowly but surely catch up; at the beginning of each content unit they are behind, but they get up to speed by the end of each unit.

claims.text: Vocabulary size is not the primary aim of schooling but is a consequence of broad knowledge and a check on school quality.
claims.source_passage: Vocabulary size is not the primary aim of schooling; it is the consequence of broad knowledge, and a convenient check on school quality and equity.

claims.text: The knowledge children gain in early grades should not be constrained by what they are able to decode from the printed word.
claims.source_passage: the knowledge children gain should not be constrained or slowed down by what they are able to decode from the printed word. That would simply intensify the Matthew Effect.

claims.text: Reading comprehension typically does not catch up with listening comprehension until late middle school.
claims.source_passage: It shows that reading comprehension typically does not catch up with listening comprehension until late middle school.

claims.text: Young children lack the cognitive bandwidth to decode printed text while simultaneously grasping substantive meaning.
claims.source_passage: The child in kindergarten, first, and second grade who is starting to decode does not always have enough bandwidth to decode printed text and also ponder and grasp its meaning.

claims.text: Schools should not try to combine early decoding with substantive texts.
claims.source_passage: The school should not try to combine early decoding with substantive texts. That obvious bit of practicality has big implications for early schooling.

claims.text: In the earliest grades, learning by being read aloud to and through talking and listening is fundamental to language progress.
claims.source_passage: in the earliest grades learning by being read aloud to and through talking and listening is fundamental to language progress and needs to receive great emphasis.

claims.text: One hour a day on decoding is more than enough time for children to learn the skill efficiently.
claims.source_passage: An hour a day on decoding has proved to be more than enough.

claims.text: Integrating fiction and various disciplines of fact in the curriculum reinforces topics and speeds up word acquisition.
claims.source_passage: Such integration of the disciplines not only reinforces the topics that are discussed and read, and written about in all subjects, but it also greatly speeds

claims.text: The integration of various disciplines (such as history, art, and literature) around shared topics significantly accelerates the growth of student vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: Such integration of the disciplines not only reinforces the topics that are discussed and read, and written about in all subjects, but it also greatly speeds up the growth of vocabulary.

claims.text: In the absence of a well-integrated curriculum, vocabulary acquisition depends on isolated word study, which is a method of limited effectiveness.
claims.source_passage: In the absence of a well-integrated curriculum, reliable vocabulary gain currently depends on isolated word study, which is a method of limited effectiveness.

claims.text: Current primary school literacy blocks devote between 16 and 33 percent of their time to isolated vocabulary drills and word study.
claims.source_passage: In the primary school, these grim exercises typically occupy twenty to thirty minutes a day in a literacy block of ninety minutes to two hours, thus taking up between 16 and 33 percent of the whole ELA curriculum.

claims.text: Word learning occurs up to four times faster when students are systematically becoming familiar with new knowledge domains compared to isolated word study.
claims.source_passage: The large amount of school time spent in direct word study is time that is not being spent on systematically becoming familiar with new knowledge domains, where word learning occurs naturally—and up to four times faster—without effort.

claims.text: Direct word study can yield at best an acquaintance with 400 words per school year, which is insufficient for college and career readiness.
claims.source_passage: Building domain knowledge is a far more efficient, accurate, and secure way of inducing word learning than word study on its own, which can yield at best some acquaintance with four hundred words in a school year. That is not a good way to arrive at the tens of thousands of words a seventeen-year-old needs to know to be college and career ready.

claims.text: National-level data from the United States, France, and Sweden show that knowledge-based curricula develop vocabulary better than skills-based curricula.
claims.source_passage: The best data we have in education—the verbal scores of whole nations, including the United States, France, and Sweden, which have massively tried the two different kinds of curriculum—show that the knowledge curriculum develops vocabulary far better than the skills curriculum does.

claims.text: Topic familiarity is the essential 'secret' to both reading comprehension and efficient word learning.
claims.source_passage: Such an integrated approach is the optimal method for increasing the knowledge and vocabulary size of students, because topic familiarity (the secret to both comprehension and word learning) is optimal.

claims.text: Incidental or unconscious word acquisition through topic familiarity is the most efficient and secure way to learn new vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: It is the method of incidental word learning (i.e., unconscious word acquisition) through topic familiarity. One learns new words automatically with the least time and effort when encountering them in a context that has become familiar.

claims.text: The technique of domain immersion is especially helpful for building the vocabularies of non-native English speakers.
claims.source_passage: Those who teach English as a second language will quickly notice that the technique of domain immersion will be especially helpful for building the vocabularies of non-native speakers.

claims.text: Systematically building topic familiarity is the most effective tool for closing the achievement gap and does not hold back advanced students.
claims.source_passage: Systematically building topic familiarity helps most those who are most behind. It is probably the most effective tool of gap closing yet devised, and it does not hold back more advanced students.

claims.text: In a rich textual environment, tier-two academic words are acquired naturally and do not require explicit instruction.
claims.source_passage: In a rich textual environment tier-two words will take care of themselves, just as they almost certainly did for the readers of this book when they were growing up.

claims.text: Competence in language is fundamentally gained through the acquisition of knowledge about things.
claims.source_passage: Competence in language is gained by knowledge of things. The best way to learn lots of words is to systematically and coherently learn lots of things.

claims.text: Words more easily yield their meaning from context when the teacher and student stay on a consistent topic.
claims.source_passage: If the teacher and student stay on topic, then, words more easily yield their meaning from context, because the larger meaning is being understood through familiarity.

claims.text: Tier-three words, which are critical technical terms within a knowledge curriculum, require brief explicit explanation by the teacher.
claims.source_passage: The teacher pauses briefly to explain the sense of critical tier-three words like seismic. It’s those critical tier-three words in the knowledge curriculum that demand brief explicit comment.

claims.text: Word learning is most effective when students are focusing on the subject matter rather than the words themselves.
claims.source_passage: When students are not thinking about learning words, they are learning words most effectively. Thinking referentially about what words mean is the way we all came to know as many words as we do.

claims.text: The only enduring method to raise academic achievement and narrow demographic gaps is to close the knowledge and vocabulary gap.
claims.source_passage: The only enduring way to raise achievement and narrow gaps between groups of students is by closing the knowledge and vocabulary gap.

claims.text: The most egalitarian school model is one that utilizes a cumulative, multiyear plan for building knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The most egalitarian school is one that follows a cumulative, multiyear plan of knowledge building.

claims.text: Knowledge is the ultimate underlying basis of standardized test scores.
claims.source_passage: Over time, this inevitably happens when a knowledge sequence is followed faithfully, because the ultimate basis of test scores is knowledge.

claims.text: If a school narrows the knowledge gap between rich and poor students, the gap in their test scores will also narrow.
claims.source_passage: If the word/knowledge gap is narrowed, so will be the gap in test scores between rich and poor.

claims.text: Any primary school, regardless of student demographics, can achieve reading scores equal to affluent areas if they implement a knowledge-based curriculum over several years.
claims.source_passage: If PS 124 in Queens, New York, composed of immigrant, poor, and racially diverse students, can consistently post scores that are equal to reading scores in more affluent zip codes, then any primary school can do the same—over a period of years.

claims.text: Impoverished students require more background knowledge rather than more skill-based drills to succeed.
claims.source_passage: these two brave women decided that what their students needed was not more skill drills but more knowledge. The students in Queens, they concluded, needed to learn the same things that children in Scarsdale knew.

claims.text: A content-rich curriculum increases student engagement and teacher collaboration.
claims.source_passage: Using the new curriculum as a foundation, we increased teacher collaboration and student engagement.

claims.text: A knowledge-based curriculum can significantly reduce student and teacher mobility within a school.
claims.source_passage: With a homeless shelter two blocks away, student mobility used to be high, but after Core Knowledge was introduced in 1999–2000, parents went to great lengths to keep their children in PS/MS 124... Ever since we adopted Core Knowledge, teacher mobility also dropped—and remains low to this day.

claims.text: High-level content is not 'too rigorous' for students because historical events and scientific discoveries are inherently interesting.
claims.source_passage: At first we feared that the content would be too rigorous... We soon discovered that all of us loved being challenged. Major historical events, scientific discoveries, and works of art are inherently interesting; with our new curriculum, teachers and children loved learning.

claims.text: Adopting a Core Knowledge curriculum can lead to a significant drop in teacher mobility.
claims.source_passage: Ever since we adopted Core Knowledge, teacher mobility also dropped—and remains low to this day.

claims.text: Rigorous academic content in history, science, and art is inherently interesting to both educators and students.
claims.source_passage: Major historical events, scientific discoveries, and works of art are inherently interesting; with our new curriculum, teachers and children loved learning.

claims.text: The background knowledge gained through a content-rich curriculum in elementary grades is sustained and extended into middle school.
claims.source_passage: The results indicated that the background knowledge they gleaned through Core Knowledge in the elementary grades was sustained and extended.

claims.text: Standard state and district instructional mandates often fail to inspire students to learn because they prioritize rote approaches over deep knowledge.
claims.source_passage: It became apparent that while the teachers were planning well and adhering to state and district instructional mandates, the students were not being inspired to learn.

claims.text: A shared knowledge sequence allows for powerful 'embedded professional development' where teachers support each other's content mastery.
claims.source_passage: By sharing their instructional plans with each other, they expanded each other’s knowledge base in all subjects. This embedded professional development was powerful.

claims.text: Content-rich schooling prepares students for citizenship, college, and diverse careers better than skills-based schooling.
claims.source_passage: Many expressed, “This is why I became a teacher—to provide students with instruction that prepares them for citizenship, college, and all sorts of careers.”

claims.text: A knowledge-rich curriculum awakens a student's innate desire to learn and fosters spontaneous academic engagement.
claims.source_passage: We were finally awakening their desire to learn. Very early on, students began asking more questions and even discussing the content among themselves. Academic debates happened on the playground.

claims.text: Inner-city primary schools often lack a spirit of community and common purpose due to a focus on individualized skills curricula.
claims.source_passage: The usual atmosphere of our inner-city primary schools is far from happy or cohesive among either teachers or students. A spirit of community built upon a common purpose is often lacking.

claims.text: Emphasizing individuality in early education is a paradox that thwarts actual individuality by failing to deliver shared knowledge.
claims.source_passage: There’s an overlooked paradox in emphasizing individuality in early years; it defeats itself. It fails to deliver the shared knowledge of the community. That cramps children’s competence and thus their freedom to develop their individual tastes and abilities. Individualism in the primary curriculum thwarts individuality.

claims.text: True individuality develops only through and in relation to the social community and its shared norms.
claims.source_passage: It’s in relation to the community that individuality develops. Only by mastering the shared knowledge and norms of the community can one diverge from those norms effectively.

claims.text: The basis of language is commonality, and individual style is built upon mastery of shared conventions.
claims.source_passage: The basis of language is commonality. Individuality comes from mastery of its shared conventions; that mastery is the foundation of an individual style.

claims.text: Ignorance of group conventions results in ineptitude and inequality rather than fostering individuality.
claims.source_passage: Ignorance of group conventions doesn’t foster individuality but ineptitude and inequality.

claims.text: Common subject matter creates a sense of common purpose that reduces alienation and misbehavior in schools.
claims.source_passage: Common subject matter sponsors a sense of common purpose in which students and teachers feel they are a part. Core Knowledge schools are happy schools. There is no sense of the alienation so pervasive in our public schools, and so productive of misbehavior.

claims.text: Schools that faithfully implement Core Knowledge principles raise achievement and narrow achievement gaps more effectively than comparison schools.
claims.source_passage: Studies have uniformly shown that schools that faithfully implement Core Knowledge–style principles have raised achievement and narrowed gaps far more effectively than comparison schools.

claims.text: The success of Core Knowledge schools validates the principle of a specific, communal, grade-by-grade curriculum rather than just a specific program.
claims.source_passage: Their widespread success is best viewed not as evidence for a program but for a principle—that of a specific, communal, grade-by-grade curriculum. I call the general principle 'lowercase core knowledge.'

claims.text: The curricular principle of specific grade-by-grade knowledge is the best-grounded and most successful in the world.
claims.source_passage: Educational research over the globe has already established that its curricular principle is the best grounded and most successful in the world.

claims.text: Core Knowledge is not a scripted program, and its specific contents are adaptable by schools.
claims.source_passage: The precise contents of the Core Knowledge Sequence are not the Tables of the Law. Core Knowledge is not a scripted program. Schools adjust the specifics.

claims.text: Individualized skills-based approaches current in American schools yield poor results and lack scientific grounding.
claims.source_passage: Those principles are far better grounded in science and in practical results than are the individualized skills approaches with uncertain subject matters current in American schools, which continue to yield poor results.

claims.text: National school systems that specify content and expertise levels for each grade level achieve the best results in achievement and equity.
claims.source_passage: PISA analysts have stated explicitly that the best results in achievement and equity are found in national school systems in which all parties—parents, teachers, and students—are made aware of the specific content and level of expertise that each student is expected to attain at each grade level.

claims.text: A systematic approach to knowledge building is more productive than an arbitrary skills approach.
claims.source_passage: Both PISA and common sense affirm that a systematic approach to knowledge building is more productive than an arbitrary skills approach with unspecified topics.

claims.text: The American educational research community has failed to conduct large-scale longitudinal studies of Core Knowledge despite its longevity and uniqueness.
claims.source_passage: Yet scientific curiosity has not induced the much-needed large study.

claims.text: Educational specialists in the United States should not state that Core Knowledge is an 'unproved' program when they have actively declined to fund or conduct the large-scale studies necessary to prove it.
claims.source_passage: In sum, educational specialists in the United States (those who accept the three disabling ideas traced in the prologue) should not continue to state that Core Knowledge is an 'unproved' program, on the grounds that they have declined to prove it.

claims.text: Core Knowledge is not merely a specific program but a curricular principle supported by an immense body of international evidence and research.
claims.source_passage: It is not a program but a curricular principle for which there is an immense body of international evidence and research.

claims.text: The small-scale studies of Core Knowledge in the US yield positive results that confirm the large-scale findings of international PISA reports.
claims.source_passage: The small-scale studies of Core Knowledge, all positive, simply confirm the large-scale conclusions of the PISA reports.

claims.text: Successful PISA nations utilize curricular sequences that share six key characteristics: they are field-tested, topic-specific, well-rounded, coherent, cumulative, and selective.
claims.source_passage: The curricular sequences of the most successful PISA nations share six key characteristics with Core Knowledge.

claims.text: Topic specificity is the most important curricular characteristic to advocate for in the United States because it ensures all stakeholders know the required level of expertise at each stage.
claims.source_passage: Topic specificity is probably the most important characteristic to insist upon in the United States, since it makes everyone—teachers, students, and parents—aware of the level of expertise that students will have gained at each point.

claims.text: Breadth of knowledge and vocabulary, including proper nouns, is critical for high reading skill and communicative proficiency.
claims.source_passage: Such well roundedness is important also in a purely practical view, since breadth of knowledge and vocabulary (including proper nouns!) is critical to high reading skill and other communicative skills.

claims.text: Broad knowledge is a critical tool for ameliorating class distinctions and achieving social equality.
claims.source_passage: Broad knowledge is also critical for ameliorating class distinctions, and placing all citizens on a more equal footing.

claims.text: The individualized and disconnected nature of current American language arts topics hinders substantive learning and vocabulary acquisition.
claims.source_passage: Topic coherence within a grade level is particularly important in the language arts class, where topics in the United States are currently individualized, fragmentary, and disconnected. Such incoherence hinders substantive learning and vocabulary acquisition.

claims.text: To be effective for word acquisition, instruction must focus on sustained topics within the same 'word fields' for two weeks or more.
claims.source_passage: Sustained topics requiring the same 'word fields' need to last two weeks or more to be effective.

claims.text: A multiyear cumulative sequence of topics is technically necessary to ensure all students are prepared for upcoming lessons and no student is denied critical knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Designing a multiyear cumulative sequence of topics is important technically to make sure that students are ready for each subject over the sequence of grade levels, assuring commonality across all classes within a grade level. No student is denied critical knowledge.

claims.text: The educational theory that any topic can be used to teach general skills is a fundamental empirical mistake that leads to failure.
claims.source_passage: The idea that any sensible topic will serve to inculcate general skills has been a fundamental empirical mistake that guarantees failure.

claims.text: Certain curricular topics, like Romeo and Juliet, are essential to social equality because ignorance of them leads to social exclusion and scorn.
claims.source_passage: Still others, like Romeo and Juliet, are essential to equality, because to be ignorant of them is to be ignorant of what others know, and thus to be scorned and excluded from comprehension and from the esteem of others.

claims.text: Shared knowledge in the elementary curriculum is the primary 'nourisher' of the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: They enhance a sense of community because everyone, not just the favored few, possesses the shared knowledge of the elementary curriculum: the great nourisher of the public sphere.

claims.text: The Washington School in Rochester, Minnesota, has posted the best test scores in the city and narrowed the achievement gap better than any other local school.
claims.source_passage: The school, the Washington School, was to be featured because it had been posting the best scores in the city, and also narrowed the achievement gap between advantaged and disadvantaged pupils better than any other local school.

claims.text: There is a massive unfilled popular demand for Core Knowledge schooling in Rochester, evidenced by a waiting list that is nearly three times the school's total capacity.
claims.source_passage: With a total capacity of 351 students in grades K–5, the school had a waiting list of over 1,000.

claims.text: It is currently extremely difficult to change basic educational ideas in the United States, even when there is significant parental demand for content-based curriculum.
claims.source_passage: This is a pretty good example of how difficult it currently is to change basic educational ideas in the United States, even with a sizeable number of parents eager to see a shift toward a more enabling, content-based curriculum.

claims.text: Effective educational changes typically originate from school leadership and teachers rather than from parents.
claims.source_passage: The effective changes in ideas that I have seen over the years have come from within—from brave principals like Valarie Lewis and adventurous teachers—more than from parents

claims.text: Parents feel helpless to influence school curriculum because they lack confidence when faced with the sophisticated slogans of skill-centric doctrine.
claims.source_passage: parents, who feel helpless and unsure in the face of the highly definite viewpoints and brilliant slogans of skill-centric doctrine.

claims.text: A school that produces large yearly gains for all students is inherently gap-closing in its social effects.
claims.source_passage: We’ve known since the Coleman Report that a school that induces large yearly gains for all students is a school that is inherently gap-closing in its effects

claims.text: The American education system is paralyzed and generally only changes in unproductive directions under the threat of standardized testing.
claims.source_passage: From afar, the American system seems paralyzed, unable to change except under threats of tests, and then in unproductive, test-prep directions.

claims.text: Parents and educators are prevented from trying knowledge-based curricula because those approaches contradict the child-centered, skill-centric doctrines that hold an intellectual monopoly.
claims.source_passage: my settled belief, is that parents and educators in low-scoring elementary schools... are prevented from trying out a knowledge-based, communal curriculum because such an approach contradicts the child-centered, skill-centric doctrines that are still an intellectual monopoly in our education world.

claims.text: Adopting a knowledge-based curriculum is hindered by psychological barriers, including resistance to changing habits and anxiety about teaching unfamiliar subject matter.
claims.source_passage: To this psychological barrier must be added two others: an all-too-human resistance to changing one’s habitual mode of activity, and an anxiety about teaching unfamiliar subject matters.

claims.text: The internal difficulties of school reform can be overcome if a new belief system is established within the school under strong leadership.
claims.source_passage: But we know from decades of experience that these inherent difficulties can be overcome if a new belief system arises within the school under strong leadership.

claims.text: Seeing direct evidence of success, such as in the Washington School or PS 124, provides educators with a necessary sense of efficacy to pursue reform.
claims.source_passage: When people see the evidence of success with their own eyes, as in PS 124 and the Washington School, they gain a sense of efficacy.

claims.text: A district-wide curriculum is necessary to allow for cooperation and shared support across schools.
claims.source_passage: A district-wide curriculum should be the aim, because the entire district can then cooperate in giving schools the support they need.

claims.text: Directly witnessing the success of knowledge-based schooling in specific institutions builds a sense of efficacy among educators.
claims.source_passage: When people see the evidence of success with their own eyes, as in PS 124 and the Washington School, they gain a sense of efficacy.

claims.text: District-wide curriculum implementation is superior to school-by-school implementation because it enables district-level cooperation and support.
claims.source_passage: A district-wide curriculum should be the aim, because the entire district can then cooperate in giving schools the support they need in putting a challenging elementary curriculum into effect.

claims.text: In many American cities, the student mobility rate is approximately 30 percent, often occurring within the same district.
claims.source_passage: In most cities, the mobility rate is around 30 percent, sometimes higher, with grave educational consequences.

claims.text: The negative educational effects of high student mobility are exacerbated by discontinuities in subject matter between different schools.
claims.source_passage: The ill effects of mobility would be much alleviated if students did not have to suffer discontinuities in the subject matters they were studying.

claims.text: A district-wide curriculum provides educational equity for mobile, disadvantaged students by ensuring curricular continuity.
claims.source_passage: A further highly important value of districtwide implementation is rarely mentioned: the movement of disadvantaged students from school to school in the middle of the year for economic reasons... The ill effects of mobility would be much alleviated if students did not have to suffer discontinuities in the subject matters they were studying.

claims.text: Standardized reading comprehension tests are primarily measures of communal knowledge and vocabulary.
claims.source_passage: A reading comprehension test is a test of communal knowledge and vocabulary.

claims.text: Test-preparation schooling has demonstrated limited utility in significantly raising reading scores over the past decade.
claims.source_passage: After more than a decade of test-prep schooling we know that test-prep has limited utility in raising reading scores.

claims.text: A well-delivered communal elementary curriculum is the necessary prerequisite for all children to succeed on reading comprehension tests.
claims.source_passage: To enable all children to possess that knowledge requires a well-delivered, communal elementary curriculum that delivers such knowledge to all. If all children master such a curriculum, they will be enabled to score well on tests of reading comprehension. QED

claims.text: Instituting a specific districtwide curriculum is the most reliable way to ensure students master the communal knowledge required for success.
claims.source_passage: the surest and best way to ensure that children in a district master a communal curriculum is for the district to institute a specific districtwide curriculum.

claims.text: Districts should implement a three-year moratorium on skills-based reading tests while transitioning to a knowledge-rich curriculum.
claims.source_passage: an imaginative superintendent could arrange to get a “research exemption” and call a three-year moratorium on skills-based reading comprehension tests.

claims.text: Locally-made content tests based on the school curriculum should be used as a more valid alternative to skills-based tests during curriculum reform.
claims.source_passage: The district could then substitute locally made content tests based on the school curriculum—in full confidence that after the moratorium, students would perform far better than before on the standard reading comprehension tests.

claims.text: Implementing a knowledge-based curriculum leads to a significant improvement in student and teacher morale.
claims.source_passage: A great improvement in the morale of students and teachers ensues, along with a great improvement in achievement and equity.

claims.text: Curriculum-based tests possess 'face validity' and are inherently fairer than skills-based tests because they test what was actually taught.
claims.source_passage: Curriculum-based tests are inherently fair. They possess “face validity.”

claims.text: The psychological resistance of Americans to imposed uniformity necessitates the creation of multiple alternative communal curricula rather than a single national mandate.
claims.source_passage: For widespread progress to be made, alternative communal curriculums will need to be created, since Americans do not like the idea of imposed uniformity. They demand a choice.

claims.text: Adopting a communal early curriculum is not a novel concept but a return to historical educational foundations.
claims.source_passage: My argument for a communal early curriculum is not, then, a novelty, nor is it an elaborate promotion of the Core Knowledge plan as being the unique way to sustain the common school in the United States.

claims.text: The common elementary curriculum in Japan and Finland is the reason parents in those nations see no functional need for charter schools.
claims.source_passage: That’s why parents in Finland and Japan have seen no reason to institute charter schools.

claims.text: The educational benefits of a 'head start' are temporary and will fade unless they are supported by a 'sustained start' through the primary grades.
claims.source_passage: Momentum is gained not just by a head start but by a sustained start. Lacking continued momentum, a mere head start slows down and fades out.

claims.text: According to the Hart-Risley research, a child from a professional family hears 45 million words by age four, compared to 26 million for a working-class child and 13 million for a welfare child.
claims.source_passage: During four years of observations, a child from a professional family would hear 45 million words, a working-class child 26 million, and a welfare child 13 million.

claims.text: Early education must be liberated from the 'exclusive trammels of the written word' to prevent the Matthew Effect from intensifying.
claims.source_passage: With it has come the realization that the education of young children needs also to be freed from the exclusive trammels of the written word.

claims.text: While isolated word study is moderately effective, its impact is too small relative to the significant amount of school time it consumes.
claims.source_passage: Isolated word study, well conducted, is moderately effective but has not significantly narrowed gaps between haves and have-nots. It takes too much time for too small an effect.

claims.text: Antonio Gramsci argued that the common school should be a 'school of humanistic formation' or a 'school of general culture.'
claims.source_passage: That was the reason Gramsci insisted that the unitary “common school” should be a “school of humanistic formation or a school of general culture.”

claims.text: District superintendents could facilitate curriculum reform by securing a 'research exemption' and a three-year moratorium on skills-based reading tests.
claims.source_passage: For instance, while the changeover is under way, an imaginative superintendent could arrange to get a “research exemption” and call a three-year moratorium on skills-based reading comprehension tests.

claims.text: The inclusion of specific cultural figures like Rosa Parks in the curriculum is a result of deliberate activism by cultural activists.
claims.source_passage: The addition of Rosa Parks to canonical knowledge has been the deliberate work of cultural activists, and more power to them.

claims.text: A content-based curriculum is 'necessarily traditional' due to the inescapable inertia of knowledge shared by hundreds of millions of people.
claims.source_passage: Because of an inherent and inescapable inertia in the knowledge that is shared among hundreds of millions of people, the Core Knowledge plan was necessarily traditional.

evidence.description: Historical argument regarding the creation of common schools as a tool for national unity in the US.
Theoretic analysis of how modern nations require common literacy and schooling to function.
The Core Knowledge Sequence (K-4 language arts) as a free, usable curriculum model.
The addition of 1,700 new words to the dictionary, largely technological, contrasted against the massive stable core of language.
Merriam-Webster Dictionary 2015 update showing new vocabulary terms reflecting modern technological and social shifts.
evidence.source_passage: Another term in the Merriam-Webster Dictionary 2015 edition is 'sharing economy,' referencing [businesses] like Airbnb or Uber... The dictionary also has the words 'click fraud' and 'clickbait'

evidence.description: The rate of change in the Webster’s Collegiate dictionary shows only a 1% shift in total entries over a decade.
evidence.source_passage: Webster’s Collegiate has introduced 1,700 new entries out of about 170,000—a change of 1 percent in a decade.

evidence.description: The absence of charter schools in high-performing nations like Finland and Japan.
evidence.source_passage: That’s why parents in Finland and Japan have seen no reason to institute charter schools.

evidence.description: The visual environment of Core Knowledge schools, specifically walls covered in student art with shared themes.
evidence.source_reference: Visitor accounts to Core Knowledge schools
evidence.source_passage: Visitors say that the first things they notice about Core Knowledge schools are their walls and halls. They are covered with student art. The art usually has a theme—a depiction of some topic or story that the students are learning in common.

evidence.description: Recalculations of the Coleman Report showing that a systematic approach to knowledge fulfills the double aim of education.
Keith Stanovich's research on the 'Matthew Effect' regarding early reading and growth in knowledge/vocabulary.
evidence.source_reference: Keith E. Stanovich, 'Matthew Effects in Reading' (1986)

evidence.description: Winship and Korenman's study correlating one standard deviation in vocabulary size with approximately $10,000 in annual income.
evidence.source_reference: Winship and Korenman, 'Economic Success and the Evolution of Schooling and Mental Ability' (1999)

evidence.description: Erika Hoff's study on how socioeconomic status affects early vocabulary development through maternal speech.
evidence.source_reference: Erika Hoff, 'The Specificity of Environmental Influence' (2003)

evidence.description: PISA data showing that nations with well-rounded primary schools and universal standards achieve superior results and narrow gaps.
evidence.source_reference: OECD, Strong Performers and Successful Reformers in Education (2011)

evidence.description: The '30-million word gap' observation, where professional families direct 45 million words to toddlers compared to 13 million in welfare families.
The Sticht Curve, showing the gap between listening comprehension and reading comprehension until approximately age 13 or 14.
Observations in Core Knowledge schools where students catch up to grade level by the end of specific content units.
Graph showing that listening comprehension leads reading comprehension in early ages but they converge as students gain literacy.
evidence.source_passage: Figure 8.1 Differences in listening and reading comprehension by age (Sticht and James, 1984)

evidence.description: Latent Semantic Analysis theory showing that word learning occurs four times faster in knowledge-rich contexts.
evidence.source_passage: The large amount of school time spent in direct word study is time that is not being spent on systematically becoming familiar with new knowledge domains, where word learning occurs naturally—and up to four times faster—without effort.

evidence.description: PISA scores of Japan and Korea demonstrate the efficacy of carefully sequenced and integrated national curricula.
evidence.source_passage: the arrangement of the best national curriculums like those of Japan and Korea, whose efficacy is manifest in the PISA scores.

evidence.description: Research in second-language acquisition shows that topic familiarity is the primary driver of word retention.
evidence.source_passage: The most efficient and secure way to learn new words has been much studied in the field of second-language learning, and its results have been shown to apply equally well to native-language learning.

evidence.description: PS 124 ELA and Math scores outperformed the New York City average by eight percentage points in 2014, even with a 100% economically disadvantaged student population.
evidence.source_reference: Valarie Lewis, unpublished report on PS/MS 124

evidence.description: PS 124 won the Dispelling the Myth Award for closing achievement gaps across all subgroup populations.
Anecdotal evidence from parents at PS 124 who described the Core Knowledge curriculum as a 'private school' education and traveled from other boroughs to keep their children enrolled.
Account of PS/MS 124 in Queens (The Yung Wing School), documenting its 15-year transformation and soaring achievement in science and social studies.
Student achievement at PS 124 described as 'almost indistinguishable from that of wealthy, white schools' despite 80% poverty.
evidence.source_reference: Karin Chenoweth, 'Successful Schools Avoid False Choices' (2009)

evidence.description: Testimony from returning high school students from PS/MS 124 who found they 'knew so much' more than their peers and could lead discussions.
The observation of academic debates occurring spontaneously among students on the playground at PS/MS 124.
Uniform positive results in schools implementing Core Knowledge principles, specifically citing Icahn charter schools and Success Academies.
evidence.source_passage: Those principles are followed by the best-performing current charter schools—in New York City in the Icahn charter schools (led by the heroic Core Knowledge early pioneer Jeffrey Litt) and in the Success Academies, and others.

evidence.description: Global PISA studies showing top rankings for nations following communal knowledge curricula.
evidence.source_passage: The PISA studies consistently place Japan, Finland, and other nations that follow lowercase core knowledge at the top of the PISA rankings in both achievement and equity.

evidence.description: A 2011 New York City study of Core Knowledge language arts in early grades showing superior results.
evidence.source_passage: In 2011, New York City ordered a moderately sized comparative study of Core Knowledge language arts for the earliest grades... It showed overwhelmingly superior results for Core Knowledge.

evidence.description: Japanese elementary curriculum consistently results in top scores across all subjects and utilizes field-testing, topic-specificity, and well-roundedness.
evidence.source_passage: For example, the Japanese elementary curriculum, which consistently scores at the top in all subjects, exhibits all those virtues.

evidence.description: The Washington School in Rochester, MN, achieved the best scores in the city and narrowed the achievement gap by using the Core Knowledge Sequence.
evidence.source_passage: The school, the Washington School, was to be featured because it had been posting the best scores in the city, and also narrowed the achievement gap between advantaged and disadvantaged pupils better than any other local school.

evidence.description: The Washington School's ranking as the only '10' out of 16 public elementary schools in Rochester by Great Schools, while others averaged 4.6.
evidence.source_passage: Great Schools’ ranking of the sixteen public elementary schools in Rochester gives just one of them a 10: the Core Knowledge Washington School... The average score of all the fifteen schools remaining after excising the Washington School from the computation is 4.6.

evidence.description: A waiting list of over 1,000 students for a school with 351 seats.
evidence.source_passage: With a total capacity of 351 students in grades K–5, the school had a waiting list of over 1,000.

evidence.description: The Coleman Report's finding regarding the gap-closing effects of schools that produce high yearly gains.
evidence.source_passage: We’ve known since the Coleman Report that a school that induces large yearly gains for all students is a school that is inherently gap-closing in its effects

evidence.description: U.S. Department of Education Blue Ribbon award granted to Washington School.
evidence.source_passage: It had received a Blue Ribbon award from the US Department of Education.

evidence.description: Success and sense of efficacy reported by schools implementing Core Knowledge.
City mobility rates cited at approximately 30 percent.
Failure of decade-long test-prep regimes to move reading scores.
Research by Stephen G. Sireci on validity theory and the reliability of content tests.
evidence.source_reference: Stephen G. Sireci, 'On Validity Theory and Test Validation,' Educational Researcher (2007)

concepts.author_definition: A system of schooling that advances a knowledgeable citizenry and social cohesion, serving as the foundation of modern democracy.
A curriculum that provides the specific knowledge required to use language effectively and participate in the public sphere.
The mastery of standard American English and the traditional knowledge base that enables success in contemporary society.
The shared set of facts, names, and concepts that members of a culture take for granted in communication; it changes slowly but can be influenced by cultural activism.
The stable core of language and knowledge that does not change quickly despite the addition of new 'slang' or technical terms.
concepts.common_alternative: Often ignored in favor of '21st-century skills' or 'modern relevance.'
concepts.source_passage: At the center of the amoeba there is a nucleus that does not readily change shape or constitution.

concepts.author_definition: The educational theory that schools should provide a shared, specific body of knowledge to all students to ensure social fairness and effectiveness.
concepts.common_alternative: The individualistic principle which tailors content to the student's unique nature.
concepts.source_passage: replaced by the far more effective and fair principle of communal knowledge.

concepts.author_definition: A curriculum that lacks coherence and prioritizes general skills over specific, domain-based content.
The cumulative advantage/disadvantage in learning where those who start with knowledge gain more rapidly, while those who start with less fall further behind.
concepts.common_alternative: Sometimes used generally in sociology to describe any cumulative advantage.
concepts.source_passage: The Effect was named after the Gospel of Matthew, which states: “For unto every one that hath shall be given, and he shall have abundance: but from him that hath not shall be taken even that which he hath”

concepts.author_definition: The concept that existing knowledge acts as an investment that enables the acquisition of new information, similar to financial capital.
The idea that early knowledge provides a sticky base to which subsequent knowledge can more easily adhere.
concepts.source_passage: The early knowledge advantage that has been gained by fortunate students is like Velcro; it is a base to which further knowledge sticks more readily.

concepts.author_definition: A term the author considers an oversimplification of the educational process, often used to justify content-light curricula.
The integration of different disciplines of knowledge (art, history, literature) so they reinforce each other and speed up learning.
The limited mental capacity of young children to perform the dual tasks of mechanical decoding and meaning-processing simultaneously.
A visual and theoretical representation of the fact that listening comprehension exceeds reading comprehension until late middle school.
The technique of staying on a specific topic or knowledge domain for an extended period (2-4 weeks) to build the familiarity necessary for incidental word learning.
The unconscious acquisition of vocabulary that occurs automatically when a student encounters new words within a familiar context.
General academic words that appear across many domains; Hirsch argues these are learned naturally in a knowledge-rich environment.
concepts.common_alternative: Words that require explicit, isolated instruction (Beck et al.).

concepts.author_definition: Subject-specific, technical vocabulary (e.g., 'seismic' or 'cuneiform') that requires brief, explicit explanation within a knowledge-based unit.
A cumulative, coherent, and multiyear plan of specific content learning that serves as the foundation for language competence and test performance.
A collaborative practice where teachers support each other's mastery of specific content knowledge and share lesson plans within a unified curriculum framework.

The educational theory that by prioritizing a child's individual interests early on and neglecting a shared curriculum, schools actually restrict the child's future freedom and capacity to develop a unique individual identity.
A sense of communal belonging and shared mission that arises when all members of a school are focused on the same clear knowledge goals.
The general curricular principle of a specific, communal, grade-by-grade curriculum, as opposed to the specific trademarked 'Core Knowledge Sequence' program.
concepts.common_alternative: Often conflated with a rigid, nationalized, or scripted curriculum.
concepts.source_passage: I call the general principle 'lowercase core knowledge.' So understood, the scientific validation of core knowledge does not depend on the willingness of the educational research community to authenticate its effectiveness with large-scale random-assignment studies.

concepts.author_definition: A rigid, highly controlled instructional format that dictates exactly what teachers must say and do, which Hirsch distinguishes from a coherent curricular principle.
concepts.source_passage: Core Knowledge is not a scripted program. Schools adjust the specifics. This distinction between scripted programs and curricular principles offers the correct research approach to the effectiveness of a coherent curriculum.

concepts.author_definition: A curricular characteristic where specific content and expertise levels are clearly defined for each point in the sequence, informing all stakeholders of what is learned and what remains.
Also known as semantic fields; clusters of related vocabulary words associated with a specific subject or topic that are best acquired through sustained instruction on that topic.
Knowledge that provides the necessary background and cultural literacy to participate in the national context and avoid social exclusion.
The dominant status of child-centered and skill-centric doctrines in American education, which prevents the consideration or adoption of knowledge-based curricula.
concepts.source_passage: because such an approach contradicts the child-centered, skill-centric doctrines that are still an intellectual monopoly in our education world.

concepts.author_definition: The belief among educators and parents that their efforts can produce successful results, often triggered by seeing tangible evidence of curricular success.
concepts.source_passage: When people see the evidence of success with their own eyes... they gain a sense of efficacy.

concepts.author_definition: Brilliant but misleading catchphrases used by the educational establishment to maintain the status quo and discourage content-based reforms.
concepts.source_passage: the highly definite viewpoints and brilliant slogans of skill-centric doctrine.

concepts.author_definition: The inherent fairness and educational productivity of a test that measures the specific content that students were actually taught in their curriculum.
concepts.common_alternative: Psychometric validity based on statistical norms across populations regardless of curriculum.

concepts.author_definition: A proposed administrative strategy allowing a district to pause standardized skills-based testing to implement and evaluate a content-rich curriculum transition.
The discrepancy in the total number of words heard by children from different socioeconomic strata (professional, working-class, and welfare) by age four.
The continued momentum of content-coherent schooling following an early 'head start' to prevent academic fadeout.
A concept from Antonio Gramsci describing a unitary common school focused on general culture rather than narrow vocational or skill training.
Traditional, shared knowledge that provides an individual with entrée to the existing structures of success and influence in a society.

concepts.term: Exclusive trammels of the written word
concepts.author_definition: The pedagogical constraint of limiting a child's learning only to what they are currently able to decode and read themselves.

cases.name: United States Common School movement
cases.description: The historical development of schools intended to unify a diverse population ('out of many one') through shared knowledge.
key_passages: Historically, the United States invented both the modern democratic nation and the common school that sustained it.

cases.name: The 1990s Curriculum Wars
cases.description: A period where Core Knowledge was criticized as elitist for being traditional, while intellectuals sought to valorize neglected cultures and deprivilege the dominant culture.
key_passages: Core Knowledge plan was necessarily traditional, and was criticized in the 1990s for being so. It appeared to perpetuate the dominance of the already dominant elements of American life.

cases.name: Finland and Japan
cases.description: Nations cited as examples where national, well-rounded curricula result in high performance and parental satisfaction without the need for alternative governance like charter schools.
key_passages: That’s why parents in Finland and Japan have seen no reason to institute charter schools.
It happens also in Finland and Japan where Core Knowledge–style schools are universal.

cases.name: Core Knowledge Schools
cases.description: A diverse group of schools (public, charter, and private) that implement a specific, content-rich curriculum and serve as a 'proof of concept' for knowledge-based education in the U.S.
key_passages: The Core Knowledge schools offer proof of concept for the knowledge-based school in the United States.
Whatever their administrative structures, they are highly similar in spirit and effectiveness.
contestable_aspects: The degree to which results are due to curriculum vs. self-selection of families

cases.name: Core Knowledge Sequence 4th Grade
cases.description: A specific implementation where the study of the European Middle Ages is integrated with Middle Ages art, literature (King Arthur), Islam, and China during the same period to reinforce vocabulary and concepts.
key_passages: In the Core Knowledge Sequence, in fourth grade, the study of the Middle Ages in Europe is accompanied by units on the pictorial art of the Middle Ages, on the King Arthur myths in literature, on Islam in the Middle Ages, and on China in the Middle Ages.

cases.name: Japan and Korea
cases.description: Nations cited as examples of the 'best national curriculums' where topics are sequenced over years and integrated across disciplines, resulting in superior PISA scores.
key_passages: the arrangement of the best national curriculums like those of Japan and Korea, whose efficacy is manifest in the PISA scores.

cases.name: United States, France, and Sweden comparison
cases.description: Whole-nation comparisons used to show that a knowledge-based curriculum produces better verbal outcomes than a skills-based curriculum.
key_passages: the verbal scores of whole nations, including the United States, France, and Sweden, which have massively tried the two different kinds of curriculum—show that the knowledge curriculum develops vocabulary far better than the skills curriculum does.

cases.name: PS 124 in Queens, New York
cases.description: A K-8 neighborhood school with a high poverty rate (80% free lunch) and proximity to a homeless shelter. Under leadership from Elain Thompson and Valarie Lewis, the school rejected skill-drill mandates in favor of the Core Knowledge Sequence starting in 1999. The school eventually closed the achievement gap, won national awards, and outperformed the NYC average on Common Core tests.
key_passages: In 1999 they decided to persuade their staff to follow the Core Knowledge Sequence.
In 2014, on New York’s tough new Common Core–aligned tests, we outperformed the New York City average.
Our test results show that we closed the achievement gap under No Child Left Behind.
contestable_aspects: The degree to which the Core Knowledge curriculum was the primary variable versus the leadership of Thompson and Lewis.
The replicability of 'working around' district regulations.

cases.name: PS/MS 124 (The Yung Wing School), Queens, NYC
cases.description: A high-poverty school that adopted Core Knowledge in 1998. The transformation led to increased teacher retention, soaring student test scores in science and social studies that rivaled wealthy districts, and a culture of academic engagement extending to the playground.
key_passages: As our implementation of Core Knowledge deepened, our fifth graders’ achievement soared.
One of the most important was that the entire school became connected.
The reality of a haphazard skills curriculum adjusted to the individual child is not as happy as its individualistic theory proposes.
contestable_aspects: Whether the success was due specifically to the content or to the leadership and collaborative culture established by the principal.

cases.name: Japan's Elementary Curriculum
cases.description: The Japanese curriculum is cited as a gold standard that is field-tested, topic-specific, and well-rounded, including arts, music, and morality alongside math and science. It consistently produces top-tier international results.
key_passages: Japan does a superb job under this criterion, and so did France in former times.
For example, the Japanese elementary curriculum, which consistently scores at the top in all subjects, exhibits all those virtues.

cases.name: The Washington School, Rochester
cases.description: A Core Knowledge school in Minnesota that received a Blue Ribbon award. It demonstrated both high excellence (top city scores) and high equity (narrowest achievement gap), leading to a waiting list of over 1,000 students.
key_passages: it had been posting the best scores in the city, and also narrowed the achievement gap between advantaged and disadvantaged pupils better than any other local school.
With a total capacity of 351 students in grades K–5, the school had a waiting list of over 1,000.

cases.name: New York City Core Knowledge Pilot
cases.description: A 20 school study (10 experimental, 10 control) of Core Knowledge. Despite 'overwhelmingly superior' results, the incoming chancellor dismissed the study as 'too small' and returned to 'balanced literacy' rather than expanding the knowledge-based approach.
key_passages: It showed overwhelmingly superior results for Core Knowledge. When the new chancellor in New York City arrived on the scene, she dismissed the study as being 'too small,' but did not propose a bigger one.

cases.name: Washington School (Rochester, MN)
cases.description: A Core Knowledge elementary school in Rochester, Minnesota, that achieved the highest test scores in the city and received a Blue Ribbon award. Despite its success and a waiting list of 1,000 students, the city had not adopted the curriculum district-wide due to systemic and ideological resistance.
key_passages: The school, the Washington School, was to be featured because it had been posting the best scores in the city, and also narrowed the achievement gap between advantaged and disadvantaged pupils better than any other local school.
With a total capacity of 351 students in grades K–5, the school had a waiting list of over 1,000.
Great Schools’ ranking of the sixteen public elementary schools in Rochester gives just one of them a 10: the Core Knowledge Washington School.
contestable_aspects: Whether the success is attributable to the curriculum or to selected teachers/students
The accuracy of the test results used for the ranking

cases.name: Rochester Today (Radio Discussion)
cases.description: A local radio show hosted by Andy Brownell and Tom Ostrom that discussed why the successful Core Knowledge curriculum wasn't expanded citywide. The discussion highlighted the myriad of ideological and political objections raised by the community.
key_passages: The topic of the radio program was to be this: Given the unfilled popular demand for the Core Knowledge Sequence, why doesn’t Rochester make that cost-free Sequence the basis for the elementary curriculum in all the schools of the city?
Ostrom had no problem recounting the objections he had heard throughout the city against the idea of adopting such a curriculum sequence citywide

cases.name: PS 124 and Washington School
cases.description: Specific schools cited as examples where the adoption of a knowledge-based curriculum produced observable success and boosted teacher/student morale and efficacy.
key_passages: When people see the evidence of success with their own eyes, as in PS 124 and the Washington School, they gain a sense of efficacy.

cases.name: Urban Mobility Patterns
cases.description: The author characterizes high student mobility (30%+) within urban districts as a primary cause of educational failure for poor students, which can only be solved by district-wide curricular uniformity.
key_passages: the movement of disadvantaged students from school to school in the middle of the year for economic reasons. This movement of poor students occurs mostly within the district—an inconvenient truth. In most cities, the mobility rate is around 30 percent...

thinkers.name: Ernest Gellner
thinkers.key_idea: Nationalism and modern nations require a common, standardized system of communication and schooling.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Gellner's work to support the idea that the common school is a necessary, if artificial, construct for the success of a modern nation.
key_passages: How artificial and necessary all this was and continues to be is well described by Ernest Gellner in his brilliant book, Nations and Nationalism.

thinkers.name: Alfred Lord Tennyson
thinkers.key_idea: The inevitability of change and the old order yielding to the new.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Tennyson to characterize the slow but real evolution of cultural knowledge.
key_passages: the old order changeth, yielding place to new

thinkers.name: Keith Stanovich
thinkers.key_idea: The Matthew Effect in reading, describing how early literacy success leads to further cognitive gains.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Stanovich's work to provide the psychological foundation for why the primary school years are the critical window for intervention.
key_passages: Psychologist Keith Stanovich explained how the Matthew Effect works with early reading, as well as its more general application to growth in knowledge and vocabulary.

thinkers.name: Betty Hart and Todd Risley
thinkers.key_idea: The word gap between social classes, showing that children from professional families hear millions more words—and higher quality words—than children from welfare families.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their research to justify the focus on vocabulary and language in early schooling as a means of social equity.
